en.extract:
The Andaman crake (Rallina canningi) is a bird species in the family Rallidae. It is endemic to the Andaman Islands of the eastern Indian Ocean.


Taxonomy and systematics
It was first described as the Andaman banded crake Euryzona canningi by Blyth in the 1863 issue of the journal, Ibis. Later, it was treated as Andamaneese banded crake Rallina canningi by Baker in 1929. Subsequently, Ripley and Ali retained Baker's scientific name for the species, while reverting to Blyth's common name.


Description

This is the largest Rallina, measuring about 34 cm in length. It has a glossy chestnut plumage, extensive bold barring on underparts, unbarred undertail-coverts, relatively bright apple-green bill and relatively long and fluffy tail; legs and feet are olive-green. It also has pale barring on wings confined to outer primaries and greater and medium coverts. Juveniles are duller and less prominently barred. Other similar looking crakes include other Rallina and ruddy-breasted crake Porzana fusca. They are smaller and do not have green bill and legs. Immature birds are duller overall with the barred areas being dark grey-brown with a chestnut tinge, narrowly banded and streaked dirty white. Juvenile birds are paler than adult. Voice is a deep croak. It utters a sharp click as an alarm call.


Distribution and habitat
Its natural habitat is marshland inside subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests, subtropical or tropical mangrove forests. It is threatened by habitat loss. It is known from North, Middle and South Andaman islands and may occur on other islands such as Great Coco or middle Coco Islands. It was formerly classified as data deficient by the IUCN, due to the lack of reliable information on its status. Later studies showed it to be fairly common in suitable habitat. Consequently, it has been listed at least concern status in 2017. BirdLife International estimates the population to range between 10,000 and 25,000 individuals.


Behaviour and ecology


Breeding
It breeds between June and August. It nests at the bottom of large trees with buttresses or under tangled undergrowth in forests. In one study, the species preferred trees such as Terminalia, Tetrameles nudiflora or Pterocarpus, possibly near waterbodies. Its eggs are similar to those of the banded crake but larger, whiter and more glossy. Both sexes share in incubation. According to Baker, the eggs are about 40 by 30 mm (1.6 by 1.2 in).


Food and feeding
Much like other crakes, it feeds on small fish, molluscs, worms and insects (beetles, grasshoppers and caterpillars). Large grasshoppers shaken about and battered before swallowing.


Threats to survival
In the 1980s, the crake was considered to be quite common on the islands; over 80 birds were snared in a square mile. It is much rare now even though there are extensive forests in the islands. Introduced predators may also pose a risk to the birds' population.


Footnotes


References
BirdLife International (BLI) (2008): 2008 IUCN Redlist status changes Archived 2008-09-14 at the Wayback Machine. Retrieved 23 May 2008.

The '''Andaman crake''' ('''''Rallina canningi''''') is a [[bird]] [[species]] in the [[family (biology)|family]] [[Rallidae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to the [[Andaman Islands]] of the eastern [[Indian Ocean]].<ref name=buceros />

==Taxonomy and systematics==
It was first described as the Andaman banded crake ''Euryzona canningi'' by [[Edward Blyth|Blyth]] in the 1863 issue of the journal, ''[[Ibis (journal)|Ibis]]''. Later, it was treated as Andamaneese banded crake ''Rallina canningi'' by [[E. C. Stuart Baker|Baker]] in 1929. Subsequently, [[Sidney Dillon Ripley|Ripley]] and [[Salim Ali|Ali]] retained Baker's scientific name for the species, while reverting to Blyth's common name.<ref name=buceros>{{cite journal|last1=Jathar|first1=Girish A.|last2=Rahmani|first2=Asad R.|title=Endemic birds of India|journal=Buceros|date=2006|volume=11|issue=2 & 3|page=14|url=http://www.birdwatching.co.in/Images/Girish%20Jathar.pdf#page=46|access-date=4 January 2015}}</ref><ref name=Ali>{{cite book|last1=Ali|first1=S. A.|last2=Ripley|first2=S. D.|title=Handbook of the Birds of India and Pakistan (Vol. 2)|date=1969|publisher=Oxford University Press|location=Bombay|pages=157–158}}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Euryzona canningi.jpg|thumb|right|Andaman crake from a chromolithograph in The Game Birds of India, Burmah and Ceylon, published in 1890]]
This is the largest [[Rallina]], measuring about 34 cm in length.<ref name=taylor>{{cite book|last1=Taylor|first1=Barry|title=Rails: A Guide to Rails, Crakes, Gallinules and Coots of the World|year=2010|publisher=A & C Black|location=London|isbn=978-1-4081-3537-2|page=76}}</ref> It has a glossy chestnut plumage, extensive bold barring on underparts, unbarred undertail-coverts, relatively bright apple-green bill and relatively long and fluffy tail; legs and feet are olive-green. It also has pale barring on wings confined to outer [[Flight feather#Primaries|primaries]] and [[Covert feather#Wing coverts|greater and medium coverts]].<ref name=taylor /><ref name=HBW>{{cite book|last1=Taylor|first1=B|editor1-last=Del Hoyo|editor1-first=J|editor2-last=Elliott|editor2-first=A|editor3-last=Sargatal|editor3-first=J|editor4-last=Christie|editor4-first=D. A.|editor5-last=de Juana|editor5-first=E|title=Andaman Crake (''Rallina canningi'')|chapter=Andaman Crake (Rallina canningi)|year=2020|publisher=Lynx Ed.|location=Barcelona|doi=10.2173/bow.andcra1.01|s2cid=216249714|url=http://www.hbw.com/node/53591|access-date=4 January 2015}}</ref> Juveniles are duller and less prominently barred.<ref name="BLI 2008">BLI (2008)</ref> Other similar looking crakes include other ''[[Rallina]]'' and [[ruddy-breasted crake]] ''Porzana fusca''. They are smaller and do not have green bill and legs.<ref name="BLI 2008"/> Immature birds are duller overall with the barred areas being dark grey-brown with a chestnut tinge, narrowly banded and streaked dirty white. Juvenile birds are paler than adult.<ref name=taylor /> Voice is a deep croak. It utters a sharp click as an alarm call.<ref name=taylor />

==Distribution and habitat==
Its natural [[habitat]] is [[marsh]]land inside subtropical or tropical moist lowland [[forest]]s, subtropical or tropical mangrove forests. It is threatened by [[habitat loss]]. It is known from North, Middle and South Andaman islands and may occur on other islands such as [[Coco Islands|Great Coco or middle Coco Islands]]. It was formerly classified as [[data deficient]] by the [[IUCN]], due to the lack of reliable information on its status.<ref>BLI (2004)</ref> Later studies showed it to be fairly common in suitable habitat.<ref name=Ezhilarasi>{{cite thesis|last1=Ezhilarasi|first1=N|last2=Vijayan|first2=L|title=Status and ecology of Andaman Crake. Submitted to Salim Ali Centre for Ornithology and Natural History, Coimbatore.|date=2008}}</ref> Consequently, it has been listed at [[least concern]] status in 2017. BirdLife International estimates the population to range between 10,000 and 25,000 individuals.<ref name="BLI 2008"/>

==Behaviour and ecology==

===Breeding===
It breeds between June and August. It nests at the bottom of large trees with buttresses or under tangled undergrowth in forests. In one study, the species preferred trees such as ''[[Terminalia (plant)|Terminalia]]'', ''[[Tetrameles nudiflora]]'' or ''[[Pterocarpus]]'', possibly near waterbodies.<ref name=Vijayan>{{cite journal|last1=Ezhilarasi|first1=N|last2=Vijayan|first2=L|title=Nest plant selection of the Andaman Crake ''Rallina canningi'', Andaman Islands, India|journal=Journal of Threatened Taxa|date=2010|volume=2|issue=11|pages=1301–1302|doi=10.11609/JoTT.o2467.1301-2|doi-access=free}}</ref> Its eggs are similar to those of the [[banded crake]] but larger, whiter and more glossy. Both sexes share in incubation. According to [[E. C. Stuart Baker|Baker]], the eggs are about {{Convert|40 by 30|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name=Ali />

===Food and feeding===
Much like other crakes, it feeds on small fish, molluscs, worms and insects (beetles, grasshoppers and caterpillars). Large grasshoppers shaken about and battered before swallowing.<ref name=Ali />

===Threats to survival===
In the 1980s, the crake was considered to be quite common on the islands; over 80 birds were snared in a square mile.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Ali|first1=Salim|last2=Ripley|first2=Dillon|title=Handbook of the birds of India and Pakistan|date=2001|publisher=Oxford University Press|location=Delhi|isbn=978-0-19-565506-3|edition=2nd}}</ref> It is much rare now even though there are extensive forests in the islands. Introduced predators may also pose a risk to the birds' population.
